name: Distributions and Moments
tagline: Recognize the distribution behind a problem (uniform, normal, exponential, Poisson) and use the moment-generating function to extract moments without integrating.

blocks.text: Most quant probability problems reduce to recognizing one of a handful of canonical distributions: uniform (geometric problems on intervals), normal (sums and limits, Black-Scholes), exponential (waiting times for Poisson processes), Poisson (rare events with fixed rate), binomial (success counts), geometric (trials until first success). Picking the right one is half the battle.

The moment-generating function (MGF) M(t) = E[e^(tX)] encodes ALL moments. The k-th moment is the k-th derivative of M at t = 0. For the standard normal, M(t) = e^(t^2/2), so the MGF method gives E[X^k] for any k without integrating. For sums of independent random variables, MGFs MULTIPLY -- a powerful trick for finding the distribution of a sum.

blocks.label: Mental model
blocks.text: First, name the distribution. Second, look up (or derive) its MGF. Third, take derivatives at t = 0 for moments. Fourth, exploit memorylessness (exponential / geometric) when the problem has 'no memory of the past'.

whenToUse: Geometric probability problems on intervals or regions (uniform).
Waiting-time problems for events with constant rate (exponential, Poisson).
Computing E[X^k] for normal random variables (MGF approach).
Convolutions / sums of independent random variables (multiply MGFs).
Anywhere you see 'memoryless' as a property of the process.
recipe: Identify the distribution from the problem structure: uniform on a region, exponential between events, normal in the limit of sums, etc.
Compute the MGF or look up the standard MGFs.
For moments E[X^k]: differentiate M(t) k times and evaluate at t = 0.
For sums X_1 + X_2 + ... of independent random variables: M_sum(t) = product of M_i(t).
Use memorylessness (P(T > s + t | T > s) = P(T > t)) for exponential waiting-time problems.
commonPitfalls: Confusing PDF with CDF -- PDF integrates to 1 over the support; CDF goes from 0 to 1.
Forgetting that E[X^k] requires differentiating MGF k times -- one fewer or one more changes the answer.
Mistaking a standard normal moment formula -- E[X^(2k)] = (2k-1)!! for X ~ N(0,1).
Applying the inspection paradox incorrectly: when you sample at a random time, you're MORE likely to land inside a long interval.
Treating 'two arrivals per minute' as 'guaranteed two arrivals' -- it's a rate, not a count.
